title: Constants, Literals, Domain Types, and Configuration · Gate Forensics
summary: Replace ambiguous magic values without creating meaningless constant noise. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a domain constant and configuration policy and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish harmless local literals from repeated domain rules, units, limits, and protocol values.
Name stable domain values by meaning and keep them near their owning policy.
Use enums, unions, literal types, branded values, or value objects for closed domains.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

# Risk-profiled repository gate portfolio
# Each gate needs purpose, command, failure fixture, owner, and suppression policy.

stimulus.title: Literal Risk case record
stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: assumption
lines.text: Use a changed debug case for gates constants literals that tests this competency: Distinguish harmless local literals from repeated domain rules, units, limits, and protocol values.. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: Every numeric or string literal must become a constant.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Distinguish harmless local literals from repeated domain rules, units, limits, and protocol values. Artifact under review: a domain constant and configuration policy.

options.text: The Literal Risk record says a domain constant and configuration policy looks right but supplies no governing condition, observation, changed case, or counterexample. This does not resolve inspect the failure record.

For inspect the failure record, apply Literal Risk without its stated preconditions, omit the a domain constant and configuration policy trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Literal Risk for a domain constant and configuration policy.
The record names the assumption, applies Literal Risk, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair
steps.instruction: Append a gates-constant-tests-1eglln: manifest entry with concrete verify plus failure, owner, or repair evidence.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Constant Tests into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
gates-constant-tests-1eglln:
  verify: [replace with a deterministic check]
  failure: [replace with the rejected condition]
  repair: [replace with a local reproduction and correction path]
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The Constant Tests structural check is only one gate. a domain constant and configuration policy must also survive the stated changed case and causal explanation.

hints: Name the Constant Tests precondition or invariant that the debug case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Use a changed debug case for gates constants literals that tests this competency: Test boundary values and downstream behavior instead of only constant equality.. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: A constant declaration cannot cause behavioral regressions.
